items.title: White House posts new presidential actions in late June
items.summary: The White House published executive orders, proclamations and a presidential memorandum across June 22–29, 2026, according to its official actions page.
items.details: The White House's official Presidential Actions page listed executive orders dated June 22 and June 25, a proclamation and a presidential memorandum on June 29, and additional nominations and appointments during the week.

items.title: DHS issues statement after Supreme Court immigration rulings
items.summary: The Department of Homeland Security said three June 25 Supreme Court rulings affirmed administration immigration policies on asylum, lawful permanent residents and Temporary Protected Status.
items.details: DHS issued a statement praising rulings in cases including Mullin v. Doe, which it said allows cancellation of Temporary Protected Status designations for Syria and Haiti, and Mullin v. Al Otro Lado, addressing asylum eligibility at the border.

items.title: U.S., Israel and Lebanon sign framework agreement in Washington
items.summary: Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced a trilateral framework agreement signed June 26 in Washington, which Iran and Hezbollah said contradicts an earlier U.S.-Iran memorandum.
items.details: The framework was signed at a State Department ceremony attended by the Lebanese and Israeli ambassadors. Hezbollah rejected the agreement. Reporting noted it had no substantial differences from the existing Israel-Lebanon ceasefire.

items.title: U.S.-Iran interim deal extends ceasefire and reopens shipping route
items.summary: President Trump and Iran's president signed an interim agreement extending the ceasefire 60 days, reopening the Strait of Hormuz and beginning negotiations over Iran's nuclear program.
items.details: Following the signing, the U.S. lifted its blockade of Iran's ports, allowing oil sales, and the deal calls for Iran's frozen assets to be unfrozen. Subsequent talks were complicated by fighting between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon.

items.title: Congress passes major housing affordability bill
items.summary: Both chambers passed what reports described as the largest federal housing affordability legislation in decades; President Trump canceled a planned signing ceremony, citing voter ID legislation.
items.details: The House gave final passage to the bill on June 23. It streamlines federal financing regulations, creates a grant program for preapproved housing designs, and removes a chassis requirement for manufactured homes. Trump canceled the June 24 signing ceremony.

items.title: House takes up FY2027 defense authorization bill
items.summary: The House Rules Committee reviewed H.R. 8800, the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2027, and more than 1,300 filed amendments the week of June 29.
items.details: The Senate was out that week while the House was in session. The Rules Committee's June 29 meeting addressed the NDAA and its amendments, including amendments related to a voting measure referred to as the SAVE America Act.

items.title: House passes KIDS Act under suspension of the rules
items.summary: The House passed H.R. 7757, the KIDS Act, in House Vote #228 on June 29 under suspension of the rules, a procedure typically used for less controversial bills.
items.details: The vote was recorded as House Vote #228 in 2026. Suspension of the rules requires a two-thirds majority and is generally used to expedite passage of measures with broad support.

items.title: Supreme Court upholds birthright citizenship
items.summary: On June 30, the Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that the Constitution guarantees automatic birthright citizenship to virtually all children born in the United States, rejecting an executive order.
items.details: Chief Justice John Roberts wrote the majority opinion, which pointed to the 1898 Wong Kim Ark decision. The executive order, issued on the first day of Trump's second term, never took effect. Justices Thomas, Gorsuch and Alito dissented.

items.title: Supreme Court expands removal power, lets Fed's Cook stay
items.summary: On June 29 the court overturned the Humphrey's Executor precedent, expanding presidential power to fire independent agency officials, but declined to allow the immediate removal of Fed Governor Lisa Cook.
items.details: In a 6-3 ruling the court found the 2025 firing of FTC Commissioner Rebecca Kelly Slaughter lawful. In Cook's case, the vote was 5-4, with Justice Kavanaugh and the three liberal justices joining Chief Justice Roberts. Justice Sotomayor dissented in the removal case.

items.title: Supreme Court upholds state mail-ballot deadline laws
items.summary: The justices upheld state laws that count mail ballots cast by Election Day but received afterward, in the case Watson v. Republican National Committee.
items.details: The decision concerned whether federal laws establishing Election Day preempt state laws permitting counting of late-arriving mail ballots. President Trump called the ruling surprising and said it makes a federal voting bill more important.

items.title: Colorado Supreme Court blocks redistricting ballot measures
items.summary: On June 29, the Colorado Supreme Court blocked three proposed ballot initiatives that would have let voters decide whether the state could use new congressional maps in 2028.
items.details: The court found all three measures, filed by a Democratic-aligned group, violated the single-subject requirement and described it as a technical decision. The initiatives aimed to counter mid-decade redistricting in other states.

items.title: States adopt new congressional maps ahead of midterms
items.summary: Following court rulings, multiple states enacted new U.S. House maps, with reports describing Republican gains in several Southern states and Democratic gains in California and Utah.
items.details: The Associated Press reported that nearly 145 million people live in states with new congressional districts for the 2026 election. New maps were enacted in states including Texas, Missouri, North Carolina, Ohio, Florida, Tennessee, Louisiana and Alabama, while California and Utah produced Democratic-leaning seats.

CA.title: Newsom signs 2026-27 state budget
CA.summary: Governor Gavin Newsom signed California's 2026-27 state budget on June 29, which his office said is balanced with no deficit this year or next.
CA.details: The budget package included Assembly Bill 109 and related budget bills. The governor's office said the plan preserves nearly $30 billion in reserves while continuing investments in state programs.
CA.source: Office of the Governor of California

CO.title: Colorado Supreme Court blocks redistricting initiatives
CO.summary: The Colorado Supreme Court blocked three proposed ballot measures that sought to let voters decide whether the state could adopt new congressional maps for 2028.
CO.details: In three opinions released June 29, the court found the measures violated the single-subject requirement. The proposals were filed by the group Coloradans for a Level Playing Field to counter mid-decade redistricting elsewhere.

FL.title: More than 120 new Florida laws take effect July 1
FL.summary: Florida officials reported that over 120 new laws signed by Governor Ron DeSantis take effect July 1, 2026, after more than 170 bills were signed this year.
FL.details: The laws include measures renaming roadways, revising the definition of habitual traffic offender, and changing school district property reporting requirements. The state's list was updated as of June 26.

GA.title: Georgia Republican leaders decline to redistrict in special session
GA.summary: On June 17, Georgia's Republican legislative leaders rejected Governor Brian Kemp's call to redraw congressional and legislative districts during a special session, citing pending litigation.
GA.details: House Speaker Jon Burns and Senate President Pro Tem Larry Walker said they would focus on tax relief and ratifying a gas tax suspension. Kemp had called the session after the U.S. Supreme Court's Louisiana v. Callais decision.

HI.title: Hawaii governor releases intent-to-veto list
HI.summary: Governor Josh Green announced on June 26 that four measures from the 2026 legislative session were placed on his intent-to-veto list.
HI.details: Of 267 bills sent to the governor, the four under consideration relate to housing leader compensation, a transfer to the state's rainy day fund, and two education measures. Green has until July 15 to issue final vetoes.

NY.title: New York legislature passes redistricting amendment
NY.summary: On June 4, the New York State Legislature passed a constitutional amendment that would allow mid-decade congressional redistricting beginning in 2028.
NY.details: The amendment would let the Legislature redraw congressional districts and remove constitutional language barring partisan gerrymandering. It must pass again in the next legislative session and be approved by voters before taking effect.

TX.title: Supreme Court allows Texas to use new congressional map
TX.summary: The U.S. Supreme Court ruled the state's new congressional map can be used in the 2026 midterms, ending for now the legal battle over Texas's mid-decade redistricting.
TX.details: The 6-3 ruling maintained the status quo after the map was challenged as racially discriminatory. The map, signed by Governor Greg Abbott in August 2025, could add as many as five Republican-leaning seats. Litigation may continue for 2028.

VA.title: Supreme Court ends Virginia redistricting fight
VA.summary: The U.S. Supreme Court rejected Virginia Democrats' emergency appeal over congressional redistricting, leaving the state's 2021 districts in place for the 2026 races.
VA.details: The decision ended efforts to revive a redistricting referendum after the Supreme Court of Virginia struck it down 4-3 in May, citing public notice rules. Several Democratic candidates suspended campaigns after the ruling.

WA.title: Washington governor signs Millionaires' Tax
WA.summary: Governor Bob Ferguson signed Senate Bill 6346, creating a tax on income over $1 million, with revenue directed to programs including free K-12 meals.
WA.details: The governor's office said less than one half of one percent of Washingtonians will pay the tax. Funded items include free breakfast and lunch for K-12 students, childcare investments, and elimination of sales tax on diapers and certain hygiene products.
WA.source: Office of the Governor of Washington

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable developments from cited sources without endorsing any party, official, or position; readers are encouraged to consult the linked sources directly.
